modules.title: Python Fundamentals

sections.heading: Variables & Memory Management
sections.content: In Python, variables are references to objects in memory, not containers. When you write x = 10, Python creates an integer object 10 in memory and x points to it.
sections.code:
x = 10
y = x        # y points to the SAME object as x
x = 20       # x now points to a NEW object
print(y)     # Output: 10
sections.tip: If interviewer asks 'Is Python pass by value or pass by reference?' — the answer is pass by object reference.

sections.heading: Mutable vs Immutable
sections.content: Immutable types (int, float, str, tuple, frozenset) cannot be changed. Mutable types (list, dict, set) can be modified in place.
sections.code:
a = 'hello'
b = a
a = a + ' world'
print(b)  # hello — NOT affected

a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a
a.append(4)
print(b)  # [1, 2, 3, 4] — IS affected!
sections.tip: Changes to mutable objects inside functions affect the original.

sections.heading: Control Flow — Beyond Basics
sections.content: Modern Python offers concise control flow patterns.
sections.code:
status = 'adult' if age >= 18 else 'minor'

# Walrus operator (:=) — Python 3.8+
while (data := input()) != 'quit':
    print(data)

# Match-Case — Python 3.10+
match code:
    case 200: return 'OK'
    case 404: return 'Not Found'
    case _: return 'Unknown'
sections.tip: Walrus operator and match-case show you know modern Python.

sections.heading: List Comprehensions
sections.content: Interviewers judge your Python level by whether you use comprehensions.
sections.code:
evens = [x for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0]

matrix = [[1,2,3], [4,5,6]]
flat = [num for row in matrix for num in row]

char_count = {ch: word.count(ch) for ch in set(word)}
sections.tip: Don't overuse — if logic has more than 2 conditions, use a regular loop.

sections.heading: Loops — enumerate, zip
sections.content: Never use range(len(list)). Use enumerate and zip.
sections.tip: range(len()) is a red flag for interviewers.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content: Two mistakes that catch even experienced developers.
sections.code:
# WRONG — mutable default
def add_item(item, items=[]):
    items.append(item)
    return items

# CORRECT
def add_item(item, items=None):
    if items is None: items = []
    items.append(item)
    return items
sections.tip: Mutable default argument bug is the #1 Python gotcha.

key_points: Mutable objects cause side effects when shared

module_quiz.question: x=[1,2]; y=x; y.append(3); print(x)
module_quiz.explanation: y=x makes both point to same list. Appending via y modifies the same object.

module_quiz.question: Which is immutable?
module_quiz.explanation: Strings are immutable. Every operation creates a new string.

module_quiz.question: What does := do?
module_quiz.explanation: Walrus operator assigns and returns value in one expression.

module_quiz.question: [x for x in range(10) if x%2==0] = ?
module_quiz.explanation: Filters even numbers from 0-9.

module_quiz.question: def f(a,b=[]): b.append(a) — repeated calls?
module_quiz.explanation: Mutable defaults are created ONCE and shared.

modules.title: Data Types & Structures

sections.heading: Strings
sections.content: Strings are immutable. Every operation creates a NEW string. Use f-strings.
sections.code:
text = '  Hello!  '
text.strip()    # 'Hello!'

name = 'Alice'
print(f'{name} is {25} years old')

reversed_str = 'hello'[::-1]  # 'olleh'
sections.tip: Three ways to reverse: [::-1], reversed(), or manual loop.

sections.heading: Lists vs Tuples
sections.content: Lists are mutable/slower. Tuples are immutable/faster.
sections.code:
x, y = (10, 20)  # Tuple unpacking

from collections import namedtuple
Emp = namedtuple('Emp', ['name', 'age'])
emp = Emp('Alice', 25)
print(emp.name)
sections.tip: Tuples can be dict keys. Lists cannot.

sections.heading: Dictionaries
sections.content: Hash tables. O(1) lookup. Use .get() for safe access.
sections.code:
email = person.get('email', 'N/A')

from collections import defaultdict, Counter
word_count = defaultdict(int)
counts = Counter('hello hello world'.split())
sections.tip: Counter preserves insertion order since Python 3.7+.

sections.heading: Sets
sections.content: O(1) membership testing. Use for deduplication.
sections.tip: Empty set: set() not {}. {} is empty dict.

sections.heading: When to Use What
sections.content: Right structure = right performance.
sections.code:
# list → ordered, changeable
# tuple → ordered, unchangeable
# dict → key-value O(1)
# set → unique, O(1) lookup
# deque → O(1) both ends
# Counter → counting
# SortedList → pip install sortedcontainers
sections.tip: sortedcontainers is third-party. Rest are built-in.

key_points: Strings immutable — every op creates new string

module_quiz.question: 'python'[::-1] = ?
module_quiz.explanation: [::-1] reverses by stepping backward.

module_quiz.question: Can list be dict key?
module_quiz.explanation: Dict keys must be hashable (immutable). Lists are mutable.

module_quiz.question: person.get('email','N/A') if no email?
module_quiz.explanation: .get() returns default if key missing.
& is intersection — common elements.

module_quiz.question: Create empty set?
module_quiz.explanation: {} creates empty dict. set() creates empty set.

sections.heading: First-Class Functions
sections.content: Functions are objects. Pass as arguments, return them.
sections.code:
result = apply(str.upper, 'hello')  # 'HELLO'
square = lambda x: x**2
sorted(people, key=lambda p: p['age'])
sections.tip: Lambda for one-liners only.

sections.heading: *args and **kwargs
sections.content: *args = tuple of positional. **kwargs = dict of keyword.
sections.tip: args is tuple, kwargs is dict.

sections.heading: Decorators
sections.content: Modify function behavior without changing code.
sections.tip: @property, @classmethod, @staticmethod are all decorators.

sections.heading: OOP
sections.content: Class variables shared, instance variables unique.
sections.tip: @property replaces getters/setters.

sections.heading: Magic Methods
sections.content: Define how objects behave with operators.
sections.tip: __len__ for containers only. __int__/__float__ for numeric types.

module_quiz.question: *args collects?
module_quiz.explanation: *args = tuple of extra positional arguments.

module_quiz.question: Decorator does?
module_quiz.explanation: Wraps function to add behavior.

module_quiz.question: @classmethod receives?
module_quiz.explanation: @classmethod gets cls (class), not self (instance).

module_quiz.question: __repr__ vs __str__?
options: repr for devs, str for users
module_quiz.explanation: __repr__ = developer-friendly, __str__ = user-friendly.

module_quiz.question: sorted(people, key=lambda p: p['age']) — lambda does?
module_quiz.explanation: Lambda provides the key function for sorting.

sections.heading: File Operations
sections.content: Always use 'with' statement.
sections.code:
with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
    for line in f: print(line.strip())

import json
with open('config.json') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
sections.tip: csv and json are built-in.

sections.heading: Exception Handling
sections.content: Catch specific exceptions.
sections.tip: Never use bare except:.

sections.heading: Context Managers & Logging
sections.content: Auto setup/cleanup. Logging > print().
sections.code:
from contextlib import contextmanager
@contextmanager
def db_conn(name):
    conn = connect(name)
    try: yield conn
    finally: conn.close()

import logging
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
logger.info('Started')
sections.tip: Logging is built-in and configurable.

module_quiz.question: 'with open()' advantage?
module_quiz.explanation: with uses context manager — auto-closes on error.

module_quiz.question: try/except 'else' runs when?
module_quiz.explanation: else runs only when try completes without exception.

module_quiz.question: open('f.txt','a') — 'a' mode?
module_quiz.explanation: 'a' appends. 'w' overwrites. 'r' reads.

module_quiz.question: Bare except: is bad because?
module_quiz.explanation: Catches KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit too.

module_quiz.question: json.load() vs json.loads()?
module_quiz.explanation: load() reads from file. loads() parses string. 's'=string.

sections.heading: Note
sections.content: numpy, pandas, requests need pip install. os, re, csv, json are built-in.
sections.code:
# pip install numpy pandas requests
import os, re, csv, json  # Built-in
sections.tip: Know which are built-in vs third-party.

sections.heading: NumPy
sections.content: 10-100x faster than loops.
sections.code:
import numpy as np
arr = np.array([1,2,3,4,5])
doubled = arr * 2
scores = np.array([85,92,78])
passed = scores[scores >= 80]
sections.tip: Foundation of all data science.

sections.heading: Pandas
sections.content: groupby, merge are interview favorites.
sections.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
df[df['salary'] > 700000]
df.groupby('city')['salary'].mean()
df.fillna(0)
sections.tip: Focus on groupby, merge, missing data.

sections.heading: Requests
sections.content: Always handle timeouts.
sections.tip: No timeout = program can hang forever.

sections.heading: Automation
sections.content: File renaming, email validation.
sections.tip: Automation scripts make great portfolio projects.

module_quiz.question: Built-in module (no pip)?
module_quiz.explanation: json is built-in. Others need pip install.

module_quiz.question: np.array([80,90,70])[arr>75]?
module_quiz.explanation: Boolean indexing returns elements where condition is True.

module_quiz.question: df.groupby('city')['salary'].mean()?
module_quiz.explanation: Groups by city, calculates mean salary per group.

module_quiz.question: No timeout in requests.get()?
module_quiz.explanation: Without timeout, waits indefinitely.

module_quiz.question: re.match() returns None when?
module_quiz.explanation: Returns None if pattern doesn't match.

sections.heading: is vs ==
sections.content: == checks value. is checks identity (same object).
sections.code:
a = [1,2]; b = [1,2]
a == b  # True — same values
a is b  # False — different objects
if x is None:  # Correct
sections.tip: Use 'is' only for None checks.

sections.heading: GIL
sections.content: Only ONE thread executes Python at a time.
sections.code:
# CPU → multiprocessing
from multiprocessing import Pool
# I/O → threading
import threading
sections.tip: GIL released during I/O.

sections.heading: Generators
sections.content: yield produces values lazily, saving memory.
sections.tip: Use for large datasets.

sections.heading: Copy
sections.content: Shallow shares nested. Deep copies everything.
sections.code:
import copy
shallow = copy.copy(original)   # Shares nested
deep = copy.deepcopy(original)  # Independent
sections.tip: list(old) also creates shallow copy.

sections.heading: Memory
sections.content: Reference counting + cyclic GC.
sections.code:
a = 5; b = 5
a is b  # True — integers -5 to 256 cached
sections.tip: Small integer caching is CPython-specific.

module_quiz.question: a=[1]; b=[1]; a is b?
module_quiz.explanation: Different list objects. is checks identity, not value.

module_quiz.question: CPU-bound tasks in Python?
module_quiz.explanation: multiprocessing bypasses GIL.

module_quiz.question: yield does?
options: Pauses and produces value, resumes on next()
module_quiz.explanation: yield pauses, returns value, resumes on next call.

module_quiz.question: copy.copy() vs deepcopy()?
module_quiz.explanation: Shallow shares nested references. Deep copies everything.

module_quiz.question: a=256; b=256; a is b → True because?
module_quiz.explanation: CPython caches small integers -5 to 256.

sections.heading: Big-O Cheat Sheet
sections.content: Every data structure choice has performance implications.
sections.code:
# list: append O(1), insert(0) O(N), x in list O(N)
# dict: key lookup O(1)
# set: membership O(1)
# deque: appendleft/popleft O(1)
sections.tip: Never use list for membership checks. Convert to set.

sections.heading: Real Example
sections.content: Wrong structure = 5000x slower.
sections.code:
# WRONG O(N*M)
[x for x in list1 if x in list2]
# RIGHT O(N+M)
set2 = set(list2)
[x for x in list1 if x in set2]
sections.tip: Always convert to set for O(1) lookup.

sections.heading: itertools
sections.content: C-optimized iteration tools.
sections.code:
from itertools import chain, groupby, permutations, combinations
list(chain([1,2], [3,4]))  # [1,2,3,4]
# groupby MUST sort first!
list(permutations('AB', 2))  # [('A','B'),('B','A')]
list(combinations('AB', 2)) # [('A','B')]
sections.tip: groupby requires sorted input.

sections.heading: collections
sections.content: Specialized containers.
sections.code:
from collections import deque, defaultdict, Counter, ChainMap
queue = deque(); queue.appendleft(0)  # O(1)
grouped = defaultdict(list)
c = Counter('aabbc'); c.most_common(2)
config = ChainMap(user_prefs, defaults)
sections.tip: deque is pronounced 'deck'. Never use list as queue.

module_quiz.question: x in my_list complexity?
module_quiz.explanation: List scans every element — O(N).

module_quiz.question: list.pop(0) vs deque.popleft()?
module_quiz.explanation: deque O(1), list O(N) — shifts all elements.

module_quiz.question: groupby requires?
module_quiz.explanation: Groups CONSECUTIVE elements — must sort first.

module_quiz.question: Counter('aabbc')['a']?
module_quiz.explanation: 'a' appears twice.

module_quiz.question: Best for checking if element exists?
module_quiz.explanation: Set provides O(1) lookup.

sections.heading: The Trifecta
sections.content: CPU → multiprocessing. I/O few → threading. I/O 1000+ → asyncio.
sections.code:
# CPU-BOUND → multiprocessing
# I/O-BOUND → threading or asyncio
sections.tip: Say: 'It depends on CPU-bound or I/O-bound.'

sections.heading: Threading
sections.content: Concurrent I/O. GIL released during I/O.
sections.code:
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
with ThreadPoolExecutor(5) as ex:
    results = list(ex.map(download, urls))
sections.tip: ThreadPoolExecutor is modern approach.

sections.heading: Multiprocessing
sections.content: True parallelism. Separate GILs.
sections.code:
from multiprocessing import Pool, cpu_count
with Pool(cpu_count()) as p:
    results = p.map(heavy_task, data)
sections.tip: Has overhead — don't use for small tasks.

sections.heading: Asyncio
sections.content: Thousands of concurrent I/O in single thread.
sections.code:
import asyncio
async def fetch(url):
    await asyncio.sleep(2)
    return f'Data from {url}'
async def main():
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch('url1'), fetch('url2'), fetch('url3')
    )
asyncio.run(main())
sections.tip: Use for 100+ concurrent I/O.

sections.heading: Anti-Patterns
sections.content: Red flags in interviews.
sections.code:
# BAD: str += in loop O(N²)
result = ''.join(words)  # GOOD O(N)

# BAD: list.pop(0) O(N)
from collections import deque  # GOOD O(1)

# BAD: type(x) == int
isinstance(x, int)  # GOOD
sections.tip: String += or list.pop(0) = junior marking.

module_quiz.question: Scraping 10K websites — best approach?
module_quiz.explanation: Web scraping is I/O-bound. asyncio/threading handles concurrent I/O.

module_quiz.question: Threading doesn't help CPU tasks because?
options: GIL allows only one thread at a time
module_quiz.explanation: GIL prevents simultaneous Python execution.

module_quiz.question: str += in loop is bad because?
options: Creates new string every iteration O(N²)
module_quiz.explanation: Strings immutable — each += copies everything. Use join().

module_quiz.question: asyncio.gather() does?
module_quiz.explanation: Runs coroutines concurrently, returns when all complete.

module_quiz.question: isinstance() vs type()==?
module_quiz.explanation: isinstance checks inheritance chain. bool is int subclass.

modules.title: Testing, Design Patterns & SOLID

sections.heading: pytest
sections.content: Industry standard testing.
sections.tip: Every function that can fail needs error case tests.

sections.heading: Mocking
sections.content: Replace real APIs with fakes in tests.
sections.tip: @patch replaces IMPORT path, not function location.

sections.heading: Design Patterns
sections.content: Reusable solutions.
sections.tip: Singleton for shared resources. Factory for multiple implementations.

sections.heading: SOLID
sections.content: Maintainable code for large teams.
sections.tip: typing.Protocol = duck typing + type safety.

key_points: Dependency Injection is key SOLID principle

module_quiz.question: assert in pytest?
module_quiz.explanation: assert checks condition. False = test fails.

module_quiz.question: Why @patch?
options: Replace APIs with mocks in tests
module_quiz.explanation: Replaces real objects with mocks.

module_quiz.question: Singleton ensures?
module_quiz.explanation: Only one instance ever created.

module_quiz.question: Dependency Injection?
module_quiz.explanation: Receive dependencies from outside, not hardcoded.

module_quiz.question: typing.Protocol for?
module_quiz.explanation: Structural subtyping — satisfy by having required methods.

sections.heading: Virtual Environments
sections.content: Every project needs its own venv.
sections.code:
python -m venv myenv
# Windows: myenv\Scripts\activate
# Mac: source myenv/bin/activate
pip install flask
pip freeze > requirements.txt
sections.tip: Never install packages globally.

sections.heading: Type Hints
sections.content: Catch bugs before runtime.
sections.tip: Not enforced at runtime — for docs and mypy.

sections.heading: Docker
sections.content: Same environment everywhere.
sections.code:
# Multi-stage Dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim AS builder
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt

FROM python:3.11-slim
COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "app.py"]
sections.tip: Multi-stage reduces 1GB → 100MB.

sections.heading: CI/CD
sections.content: Auto-test on every push.
sections.code:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI
on: push
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
    - run: pytest -v
sections.tip: Set up CI/CD on Day 1.

sections.heading: Profiling
sections.content: Measure before optimizing.
sections.code:
import timeit
t = timeit.timeit('[x**2 for x in range(1000)]', number=10000)

import cProfile
cProfile.run('slow_function()')

breakpoint()  # Python 3.7+ debugger
sections.tip: timeit for snippets, cProfile for programs.

module_quiz.question: pip freeze > requirements.txt?
module_quiz.explanation: Lists all packages with versions for recreation.

module_quiz.question: Type hints enforced at runtime?
options: No — docs and static analysis only
module_quiz.explanation: Completely ignored at runtime. For mypy and IDEs.

module_quiz.question: Multi-stage Docker build?
module_quiz.explanation: Build stage installs, production stage copies only needed files.

module_quiz.question: CI/CD on git push?
module_quiz.explanation: Auto-runs tests, linters, checks on every push.

module_quiz.question: cProfile used to?
module_quiz.explanation: Shows which functions take most time.

quiz.question: Add to END of list?
quiz.explanation: append() adds one element at end

quiz.question: [i for i in range(5) if i%2==0]?
Generator is?
quiz.explanation: yield = one value at a time

quiz.question: [lambda i=i: i for i in range(5)]?
Counter('abracadabra').most_common(2)?
quiz.explanation: Insertion order tie-break: b before r

quiz.question: Python GC?
quiz.explanation: Ref counting + cyclic for circular refs

quiz.question: @patch?
quiz.explanation: Temp replaces with mocks in tests
